articles.label: COUNTY WATCH
articles.headline: Escambia's short-term rental crackdown enters second year with few citations, mounting complaints
articles.dek: Code enforcement issued just 14 violations in 2025 despite hundreds of resident complaints. Property owners say the rules are unclear. Neighbors say nothing has changed.
brief: Escambia County code enforcement issued 14 short-term rental violations in 2025, despite receiving 347 complaints from residents
The county's STR ordinance requires registration, safety inspections, and limits on occupancy, but enforcement remains complaint-driven
Property owners cite confusing permit requirements while neighbors report persistent noise, parking, and trash problems
articles.body:
The house on Galvez Drive sits quiet on a Tuesday afternoon. No cars in the driveway. No music bleeding through the windows. But on weekends, neighbors say, it transforms.

"Friday night, you've got eight, ten cars parked up and down the street," said Marcus Delacroix, who has lived three doors down for 22 years. "People out back until 2 a.m. Trash cans overflowing Monday morning. Then it's quiet again until the next batch shows up."

Delacroix filed four complaints with Escambia County code enforcement in 2025. He received form-letter responses. The rental continues to operate.

His experience reflects a broader pattern across unincorporated Escambia County, where a short-term rental ordinance adopted in March 2024 has produced few tangible results. County records obtained by The Flightline show code enforcement received 347 STR-related complaints last year. Officers issued 14 violations.

An ordinance with teeth—on paper

The regulations arrived after years of resident complaints and pressure from neighborhood groups in Perdido Key, Innerarity Point, and Big Lagoon. The ordinance requires property owners to register their rentals with the county, submit to fire safety inspections, maintain liability insurance, and limit overnight occupancy based on bedroom count.

Violations can carry fines up to $500 per day. Repeat offenders risk having their registration revoked.

But enforcement has proven difficult. The county's code enforcement division has seven officers covering 661 square miles. Short-term rental complaints compete with overgrown lots, unpermitted construction, and junk vehicles.

"We respond to every complaint," said county code enforcement supervisor Janet Morales. "But we have to witness the violation or document clear evidence. Someone telling us there were too many cars last Saturday isn't enough to cite."

Morales said several of the 14 violations issued in 2025 involved unregistered properties. Others cited fire safety deficiencies discovered during inspections. None involved occupancy limits.

Confusion on compliance

The county's STR registry lists 412 active permits as of March 2026. Industry estimates suggest at least 600 short-term rentals operate in unincorporated Escambia County, though precise numbers are difficult to verify. Platforms like Airbnb and VRBO do not share listing data with local governments unless compelled by subpoena.

Property owners who have attempted to register describe a process that varies depending on which county employee answers the phone.

David Nguyen purchased a three-bedroom house near Big Lagoon State Park in 2024, intending to rent it on Airbnb. He said he spent four months trying to obtain his STR permit.

"I got different answers from different people," Nguyen said. "One person said I needed a fire inspection first. Another said I needed the permit before I could schedule the inspection. I finally got it sorted out, but I can see why people just skip the whole thing."

The county's development services department acknowledged that staffing turnover created inconsistencies in 2025. A new coordinator position was filled in January.

Neighbors want action

For residents like Delacroix, the bureaucratic explanations ring hollow.

"I don't care about their staffing problems," he said. "I care that my neighborhood has turned into a hotel district and nobody's doing anything about it."

The Innerarity Point Homeowners Association submitted a petition to the county commission in February with 89 signatures requesting stricter enforcement. Association president Linda Stovall told commissioners that several longtime residents have put their homes on the market.

"People moved here for peace and quiet," Stovall said during public comment. "They didn't sign up to live next to a party house."

Commissioner Jeff Bergosh, whose district includes much of the affected coastal area, said he shares residents' frustrations but noted the county faces legal constraints.

"Florida law limits what we can do," Bergosh said. "The state preempted a lot of local authority on short-term rentals back in 2011. We're working within a narrow window."

The 2011 preemption law prevents local governments from banning vacation rentals outright. A 2014 amendment restored some regulatory authority, allowing counties to impose safety and registration requirements. But the legal boundaries remain contested, and several Florida municipalities have faced lawsuits from property owners challenging local restrictions.

What comes next

County staff presented an enforcement update to the Board of County Commissioners on April 3. They proposed adding one code enforcement officer dedicated to STR complaints and launching a public education campaign aimed at property owners.

The board took no action but directed staff to return in May with a cost analysis.

Meanwhile, the complaints keep coming. Code enforcement logged 31 STR-related calls in March alone.

Delacroix isn't optimistic. He's considering selling the house his father built in 1978.

"I never thought I'd leave," he said. "But what's the point of staying if the county won't protect the neighborhood?"

The Flightline filed public records requests for all STR complaints and enforcement actions from 2024-2026. Responsive documents are available at theflightline.com/documents.

articles.label: Development
articles.headline: The $47 million question: What happens to the old Pensacola News Journal building?
articles.dek: The downtown site has sat vacant for three years. A developer's option expires in June. City Hall isn't talking about a backup plan.
brief: The former Pensacola News Journal building on Romana Street has been vacant since 2023, when Gannett relocated operations to a smaller facility
Jacksonville-based Vestcor Companies holds a purchase option on the 2.1-acre site that expires June 30, but has not announced financing or construction timelines
City of Pensacola officials declined to comment on contingency plans if the $47 million mixed-use project falls through
articles.body:
The windows are dark on Romana Street. Have been for three years now. The old Pensacola News Journal building sits like a gap in a smile, 40,000 square feet of newsprint history slowly becoming downtown's most expensive question mark.

Walk past on a Tuesday afternoon and you'll see what $47 million in promised development looks like when it stalls. Faded construction renderings still hang in the ground-floor windows. A security guard makes his rounds. Pigeons have claimed the loading dock where delivery trucks once idled at 4 a.m.

Vestcor Companies, the Jacksonville developer that signed a purchase option with building owner Gannett Co. in late 2024, has until June 30 to close on the property or walk away. The company's plans called for 156 market-rate apartments, 12,000 square feet of ground-floor retail, and a rooftop restaurant with views of Pensacola Bay.

That was eighteen months ago. Since then: silence.

A timeline with gaps

Vestcor president Chad Rosier did not respond to three interview requests over two weeks. A company spokesperson confirmed by email that Vestcor "remains interested in the Pensacola market" but declined to address financing status, construction timelines, or whether the company intends to exercise its option.

Gannett, which still owns the building, referred questions to Vestcor.

At Pensacola City Hall, the response was equally thin. City spokesperson Kaycee Lagarde said the project "remains in the private development phase" and that the city has no formal role until a developer submits permit applications.

Asked whether city officials have discussed contingency plans if the Vestcor deal collapses, Lagarde wrote: "We don't have anything to share on that."

Pensacola Mayor D.C. Reeves did not respond to a request for comment.

The math problem

The Vestcor proposal arrived during a different moment in the capital markets. In fall 2024, developers were still riding the tail end of cheap money. By spring 2025, construction financing for multifamily projects had tightened considerably. Interest rates climbed. Insurance costs in coastal Florida continued their vertical trajectory.

Several developers active in downtown Pensacola described the current environment as "wait and see." None would speak on the record about the Vestcor project specifically.

"The deals that penciled two years ago don't pencil today," said one developer who has completed three projects in the downtown core. "You're either bringing more equity, getting creative on the financing, or you're not building."

The 2.1-acre PNJ site presents particular challenges. The existing structure—built in 1973 and expanded in 1986—would require significant demolition costs before any new construction could begin. Environmental assessments conducted in 2023 identified petroleum contamination from the building's old press operations, though remediation was described as "manageable" in Gannett's disclosure documents.

Escambia County Property Appraiser records list the current assessed value at $2.1 million, though that figure reflects the building's condition rather than the land's development potential.

What the neighbors think

Three blocks east, the Studer Community Institute occupies renovated historic buildings that once housed downtown's maritime industries. Quint Studer, the philanthropist and entrepreneur who has invested heavily in downtown revitalization over the past decade, declined to comment on the PNJ site specifically.

"I don't have any insight into that project," Studer said in a brief phone conversation.

Business owners along Palafox Street offered a range of views. Some expressed frustration at the prolonged vacancy. Others shrugged.

"Something will happen eventually," said Marcus Webb, who owns a coffee shop two blocks from the site. "Everything downtown moves slower than you think it will. Then it moves all at once."

Webb opened his shop in 2021, during the pandemic. He's seen three other storefronts on his block turn over since then. Two are occupied. One is not.

"The building's an eyesore," he said. "But I've got my own problems to worry about."

What happens next

June 30 will arrive in 81 days. If Vestcor exercises its option, the company will have committed to purchasing the property—though construction timelines would remain uncertain. If Vestcor walks, Gannett will be back where it started: holding a vacant building in a market that has grown more cautious.

The site's location—between the bay and the Palafox commercial corridor—makes it valuable in theory. In practice, valuable land requires someone willing to write large checks against uncertain returns.

Downtown Pensacola has seen this before. The old Seville Tower site sat vacant for years before Innisfree Hotels broke ground on its current project. The Maritime Park development required multiple false starts and a change in city leadership before construction began.

For now, the PNJ building waits. The pigeons don't seem to mind.

The Flightline will continue to follow this story as the June deadline approaches. Tips can be sent to tips@theflightline.com.

articles.label: Military
articles.headline: Blue Angels announce 2027 homecoming show dates as base prepares for largest crowd since 2019
articles.dek: The July event could draw 200,000 spectators. Traffic plans remain a work in progress, and Pensacola Beach residents are already asking questions.
brief: The Blue Angels homecoming air show is scheduled for July 11-12, 2027, marking the first full-capacity event since the COVID-reduced 2021 show
NAS Pensacola officials estimate up to 200,000 spectators over the two-day event, with base access procedures still under development
Pensacola Beach residents raised traffic and parking concerns at a Gulf Breeze City Council meeting this week
articles.body:
The F/A-18 Super Hornets will scream over Pensacola Bay the second weekend of July 2027. Naval Air Station Pensacola confirmed the dates Friday morning, setting in motion what officials expect to be the largest homecoming show since 150,000 people packed the base in 2019.

The July 11-12 event comes as the Blue Angels enter their second full season flying the Super Hornet, the larger and louder aircraft that replaced the legacy Hornets in 2021. For the roughly 200,000 spectators NAS Pensacola is projecting, the show will be free. Getting there is another matter.

"We're still working through the traffic management plan with our partners," said Lt. Cmdr. Brian Hayes, NAS Pensacola's public affairs officer, during a Friday press briefing. "The goal is to have detailed guidance out by January."

That timeline concerns some local officials. The 2019 homecoming show created traffic backups stretching from the main gate on Navy Boulevard to I-110, with some attendees reporting two-hour waits to enter the base. The 2021 and 2022 shows operated at reduced capacity due to COVID-19 protocols, and the 2023 through 2026 events drew smaller crowds that didn't stress the road network as severely.

Beach residents want answers now

At Tuesday's Gulf Breeze City Council meeting, three Pensacola Beach residents asked what the city was doing to prepare for spillover traffic and parking. The beach itself won't host any official show activities, but the Pensacola Bay area becomes an informal viewing zone during Blue Angels performances, with boaters anchoring offshore and spectators lining the sound-side parks.

"Last time we had a big homecoming, people parked on Via de Luna for a mile in both directions," said Karen Ashworth, a Pensacola Beach resident who addressed the council during public comment. "The fire department couldn't get through. That can't happen again."

Gulf Breeze City Manager Samantha Abell said the city has requested a seat at NAS Pensacola's traffic planning meetings but has not yet received confirmation. Santa Rosa County, which has jurisdiction over Pensacola Beach, did not respond to a request for comment.

Escambia County Administrator Wes Moreno said his office is coordinating with the Navy and expects to begin public outreach this fall. "July 2027 sounds far away, but it's really 15 months," Moreno said Thursday. "We need residents to understand that their input matters, and we need to hear it early."

A post-pandemic test

The 2027 homecoming will be the first true stress test of NAS Pensacola's crowd management since the base tightened security procedures following the December 2019 shooting that killed three sailors. The attack, which occurred just weeks before the base was set to finalize plans for the 2020 homecoming show, led to enhanced screening at all entry points.

Those security measures remain in place. Every vehicle entering the base for the air show will be subject to inspection, and all adult attendees will need to present valid identification. The base is exploring a pre-registration system that could speed entry for those who submit information in advance, though Hayes said no decisions have been finalized.

"We're balancing two priorities," Hayes said. "We want this to be accessible to the community. But force protection is not negotiable."

The 2019 show used a park-and-ride system from several off-base lots, with shuttle buses ferrying spectators to the flight line. Hayes indicated a similar approach is likely for 2027 but declined to confirm specific locations.

Economic stakes

Visit Pensacola, the tourism marketing arm for Escambia County, estimates the 2019 homecoming show generated $22 million in direct visitor spending. Hotels within 10 miles of the base sold out weeks in advance, and restaurants reported their busiest weekend of the year.

"This is one of the signature events for our destination," said Darien Schaefer, Visit Pensacola's president and CEO. "We're already fielding inquiries from people planning trips."

Hotel operators on Pensacola Beach say they've seen early booking activity for the July 2027 weekend, though rates have not yet been set. The Margaritaville Beach Hotel and the Holiday Inn Resort, the two largest properties on the beach, declined to comment on occupancy projections.

The Blue Angels flight demonstration squadron has called NAS Pensacola home since 1949. The team performs roughly 60 shows per year at locations across North America, but the homecoming show draws the largest and most loyal crowds. For many Pensacola families, attendance is a generational tradition.

The 2027 show will feature the full Blue Angels demonstration, civilian aerobatic performers, static aircraft displays, and military equipment exhibitions. Gates will open at 8 a.m. both days, with flying scheduled to begin at 11 a.m. The Blue Angels typically take the sky around 2 p.m.

NAS Pensacola said additional details, including performer lineups and access procedures, will be released in phases over the coming year.
